Curriculum Digests Newsletter

Chapter 1

The Skills We Will Teach

⏱️ 45 minutes

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • Understand why the PMM role is being redefined
  • Assess your current relationship with AI tools
  • Benchmark your skills against the 10X PMM framework
  • Preview the competencies this curriculum develops

The Headlines That Spooked Wall Street

In March 2026, Anthropic demoed Claude Cowork — and software stocks dropped 8% in a single day. The headlines were brutal:

"Wall Street Is Convinced AI Will Kill SaaS"
Fortune

When Anthropic demoed Claude Cowork in March 2026, investors panicked. The demo showed an AI agent completing complex workflows that normally required multiple SaaS tools and human operators. Within hours, software stocks shed 8% of their value. The message was clear: if an agent can do the work, why pay for the seat?

"Is SaaS Dead? Rethinking the Future of Software"
IDC

IDC's analysis goes deeper than the panic. Their research shows that by 2028, 70% of software vendors will abandon pure seat-based pricing. The shift isn't about software disappearing — it's about how value gets measured. Consumption, outcomes, capability metrics. The subscription model that defined the last decade is being renegotiated.

"The Great SaaS Unbundling: Why AI Will Destroy Half the Industry"
Uncover Alpha

This piece makes a stark prediction: half of today's SaaS companies won't survive the transition. The thesis? Many products exist only because automation was hard. When AI agents can stitch together workflows across tools, the "integration tax" that justified many platforms evaporates. The survivors will be systems of record with real data moats — not point solutions with pretty UIs.

The fear is real. But is it right?

The Counter-Narrative

While panic spread, the data told a different story:

"Enterprise Tech Spending to Cross $6 Trillion in 2026"
Computerworld

Here's the number that should calm the panic: $6.15 trillion. That's Gartner's forecast for global IT spending in 2026, up 10.8% year-over-year. AI infrastructure is driving the surge, but the money is flowing through existing platforms, not around them. Enterprises aren't ripping out SAP to let agents figure it out — they're building agents that work with SAP.

"Enterprise Software Spend Will Grow 15.2% in 2026"
Gartner via SaaStr

Software specifically — not just IT overall — will grow faster than any other segment. 15.2% is a stunning number for a "dying" industry. But look closer: the growth is shifting. AI-native capabilities, agentic workflows, outcome-based pricing. The money is still flowing to software. It's just flowing to a different kind of software.

"Global IT Spend to Exceed $6 Trillion"
CIO Dive

CIO Dive's coverage emphasizes that enterprises are accelerating, not retreating. AI budgets are additive — layered on top of existing software investments, not replacing them. The CIOs they interviewed aren't asking "how do we replace our ERP?" They're asking "how do we make our ERP smarter?"

Both narratives are right. Software isn't dying — but the way it's discovered, evaluated, and bought is being completely rewritten.

The PMMs who understand this shift will thrive. The ones who don't will wonder what happened.

SAP's Position

Here's what most people miss: SAP has the biggest data moat in enterprise.

  • Decades of structured business data across finance, supply chain, HR, and CX
  • The transactional backbone of the Global 2000
  • A platform where AI agents actually have something meaningful to work with

This is the best position in the industry for the AI era. But capturing it requires radical change in how we think about product marketing.

The playbook that worked in 2020 won't work in 2026.

Your World Has Changed

Here's the uncomfortable truth: the job you were hired for isn't the job anymore.

Five years ago, product marketing meant owning the narrative. You crafted positioning. You wrote messaging docs. You built sales decks and launch plans. Success meant great content delivered to the right audiences at the right time.

That world assumed humans controlled the discovery process. Buyers would Google, land on your website, download your whitepaper, and enter your funnel. You controlled the story because you controlled the touchpoints.

Now? An AI agent summarizes your positioning in three bullet points — alongside your three closest competitors. A buyer asks Claude to "compare enterprise data platforms" and gets a synthesis you never approved. The narrative isn't yours anymore. It's being generated in real-time by systems that don't read your messaging framework.

This doesn't mean product marketing is dead. It means the skills that matter have shifted:

Then Now
Write messaging docs Shape what AI surfaces
Create sales decks Enable AI-assisted selling
Launch and wait for leads Build always-on visibility
Own the narrative Influence the synthesis
Compete on content volume Compete on signal quality

This isn't about adding AI tools to your workflow. It's about fundamentally rethinking what product marketing is in an AI-mediated world.

Two Assessments

Before we dive into new skills, we need to establish your baseline. These two assessments measure different dimensions of readiness.

🤖

Where Do You Sit?

This assessment maps your relationship with AI — not your technical skills, but your working posture. Are you a Doer, a Framer, a Partner, or something else? Based on Helen Edwards' Cognitive Sovereignty framework.

Why it matters: Your attitude toward AI determines how quickly you'll adapt. This assessment helps you see where you actually stand.

~7 minutes
Take Assessment →
📊

10X PMM Scorecard

This assessment benchmarks your functional skills across the competencies that drive outsized impact: positioning, competitive strategy, GTM execution, sales enablement, and customer marketing.

Why it matters: Knowing your gaps is the first step to closing them. This scorecard gives you a baseline we'll reference throughout the curriculum.

~8 minutes
Take Scorecard →

The New PMM Skill Stack

This curriculum develops six core competencies. Each represents a fundamental shift in how product marketing works in the AI era — and maps to specific activities in the Pragmatic framework.

1. AI Visibility

Getting discovered by agents and LLMs

The AI Shift: When buyers ask ChatGPT or Perplexity to "recommend enterprise data platforms," your website isn't the first stop anymore. AI agents crawl structured data, entity relationships, and authoritative content. If you're not present in training data and real-time sources, you're invisible.

What Changes:

  • SEO becomes GEO (Generative Engine Optimization)
  • Schema.org markup becomes critical infrastructure
  • Content authority matters more than content volume

Pragmatic Competencies Affected: Market Research & Analysis, Competitive Intelligence, Content Strategy, Digital Presence

2. Position Defense

Messaging that survives summarization

The AI Shift: Your carefully crafted 3-paragraph positioning gets reduced to one bullet point by Claude. Buyers see AI-generated comparisons, not your messaging framework. Your differentiation must be so clear it survives compression.

What Changes:

  • Positioning must be machine-parseable
  • Differentiation needs crisp, quotable claims
  • Message testing now includes "AI summarization survival"

Pragmatic Competencies Affected: Positioning & Messaging, Competitive Differentiation, Value Proposition Development, Analyst Relations

3. Technical Enablement

Arming champions for deep evaluation

The AI Shift: Champions use AI to accelerate technical due diligence. They ask agents to compare architectures, find security documentation, and surface integration requirements. Your technical content must be AI-readable and comprehensive.

What Changes:

  • Technical docs need structured, queryable formats
  • Demo environments should be agent-accessible
  • POC content must answer the questions AI will ask

Pragmatic Competencies Affected: Sales Enablement, Technical Content, Demo Strategy, Solution Architecture Support

4. Commercial Strategy

Pricing, packaging, and value articulation

The AI Shift: AI can model TCO, compare pricing structures, and flag unfavorable terms instantly. Procurement teams use agents to negotiate. Your commercial model must withstand automated scrutiny.

What Changes:

  • Pricing transparency becomes competitive advantage
  • Packaging must have clear logical structure
  • Value metrics need quantifiable proof points

Pragmatic Competencies Affected: Pricing Strategy, Packaging & Bundling, Business Case Development, ROI Modeling

5. Committee Navigation

Multi-stakeholder deal orchestration

The AI Shift: Every committee member now has an AI research assistant. The CFO asks Claude about vendor risk. The CTO queries implementation timelines. Each stakeholder arrives with AI-synthesized concerns you didn't anticipate.

What Changes:

  • Persona content must address AI-surfaced objections
  • Stakeholder mapping includes "what will they ask AI?"
  • Deal support requires real-time intelligence

Pragmatic Competencies Affected: Buyer Personas, Sales Plays, Objection Handling, Executive Engagement

6. Lifecycle Marketing

Adoption, expansion, and advocacy

The AI Shift: Customers use AI to optimize their usage, find alternatives, and benchmark renewals. Your customer marketing must proactively surface value before they ask an agent "should we renew?"

What Changes:

  • Adoption content becomes always-on, not campaign-driven
  • Expansion signals need AI-powered detection
  • Advocacy programs must generate AI-discoverable proof points

Pragmatic Competencies Affected: Customer Marketing, Adoption & Onboarding, Expansion Strategy, Reference & Advocacy Programs

Each chapter in this curriculum maps to one of these competencies — giving you the frameworks, exercises, and assessments to master the new PMM skill stack.
